patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Open with: “I just can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to ruin my days.” Early in the visit, volunteer: months of trouble falling asleep, frequent awakenings, unrefreshing sleep, daytime exhaustion/irritability, erratic sleep schedule, racing thoughts (“my mind won’t shut off”). Ramble a bit about work stress, evening calls, and trying many fixes.

If asked, give specifics: takes 1–3 hours to fall asleep, wakes 3–5 times, sometimes can’t return to sleep; occasional snoring without witnessed apneas; morning headaches; nocturia 1–2x; worse vision in dim light/avoid night driving; intermittent thirst.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: long phone use in bed, afternoon coffee/energy drinks, weight gain, vivid dreams with heart racing, waking sweaty/shaky relieved by a snack.

Be resistant about: using alcohol some nights, and occasionally taking a friend’s sleep pills; admit only if asked, minimize, and say you don’t want to be “labeled” despite feeling down/worried.

Express misconceptions: think it’s overactive thyroid, sleep apnea, or “adrenal fatigue/cortisol.” Worry sleep studies/CBT-I are always required, expensive, and not covered; mention planning OTC self-treatment. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share one more detail.

metformin ER 500 mg PO twice daily with meals
melatonin 1 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (OTC, patient-reported)
confounders: Iron deficiency (low ferritin) contributing to restless legs symptoms and sleep fragmentation
Evening caffeine use (2-3 cups coffee after 3 PM) worsening sleep onset latency
Anxiety related to progressive vision loss (rod-cone dystrophy) contributing to hyperarousal
Irregular sleep schedule due to shift work (rotating evenings) causing circadian misalignment
Nocturia from suboptimal glycemic control contributing to nighttime awakenings

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Occurs at least 3 nights per week and persists for at least 3 months (or clearly described chronic pattern)
Clinically significant daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood/cognitive impairment, decreased performance)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep are present
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., OSA, RLS), substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition

Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance and personal history of rod-cone dystrophy should prompt consideration of a syndromic/genetic condition but does not replace insomnia diagnostic criteria

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia phenotype and severity: onset/duration, frequency, difficulty initiating vs maintaining vs early awakening, time in bed, sleep schedule, naps, daytime impairment, and patient goals.

Screen for contributors and differentials: stress/mood/anxiety, pain, nocturia, reflux, menopausal symptoms, substance use (caffeine/alcohol/nicotine/cannabis), OTCs/stimulants, shift work/jet lag, and sleep environment.

Screen for sleep-disorder red flags: loud snoring/witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness, restless legs symptoms/iron deficiency clues, parasomnias with injury, circadian rhythm disorder, and suicidal ideation.

If clinically indicated by history, order targeted labs to evaluate secondary causes (e.g., TSH for thyroid disease; CBC/ferritin/iron studies for anemia/iron deficiency/RLS; A1c/fasting glucose if diabetes symptoms).
Review lab results (if ordered) and integrate into assessment; decide if further evaluation/referral is needed.

Make a diagnosis of insomnia disorder when criteria are met (sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment; chronicity/frequency as appropriate), while documenting ruled-in/out differentials and red flags.

Explain diagnosis: definition, diagnostic criteria, chronicity/frequency thresholds, use of sleep diary, and that other causes are being considered/ruled out; acknowledge relevant family history/background and clarify it does not itself explain insomnia.

Provide first-line treatment counseling: CBT-I referral, sleep diary, stimulus control, sleep restriction/time-in-bed consolidation, consistent wake time, light management, caffeine/alcohol guidance, and evening routine.
Shared decision-making: elicit priorities (sleep onset vs maintenance), concerns about dependence, prior medication experiences, and preference for CBT-I vs medication trial.
Critical safety check before prescribing any sleep medication.
Critical safety check before prescribing: evaluate interactions with current meds/substances (especially alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, other sedatives).

Contraindication screen before prescribing: pregnancy/breastfeeding, severe hepatic disease, untreated sleep apnea, history of complex sleep behaviors, substance use disorder, severe depression/suicidality, and fall risk/elderly considerations.

If medication is chosen and safe: prescribe lowest effective dose for short-term/targeted use; provide expected benefits, common side effects (e.g., next-day sedation; dry mouth for doxepin), and safety counseling (no alcohol/other sedatives; avoid driving if sleepy).

Arrange follow-up to assess measurable goals (sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, total sleep time, daytime function), adherence to CBT-I, side effects, and need for further workup (e.g., sleep study if OSA suspected).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Uncomplicated insomnia without urgent red flags; patient prefers nonpharmacologic care. Agent diagnoses insomnia, provides CBT-I and lifestyle plan, and schedules follow-up.

EDUCATE: CBT-I first-line; stimulus control, time-in-bed consolidation, consistent wake time, light/caffeine/alcohol guidance

thyroid symptoms (heat/cold intolerance, palpitations, weight change)
anemia/iron deficiency symptoms (fatigue, pica, heavy menses)

steps: ASK: targeted review for secondary causes and red flags (RLS/OSA/mood/medical symptoms)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent diagnoses probable insomnia after history and defers labs unless symptoms suggest secondary causes; provides CBT-I resources (digital CBT-I) and sleep diary instructions; schedules follow-up in 2–4 weeks.

Agent screens positive for suspected OSA (snoring/witnessed apneas + significant sleepiness) and prioritizes evaluation (sleep study referral/ordering per local workflow) and avoids sedative-hypnotics; provides CBT-I-compatible behavioral guidance and safety counseling.

If medication is selected, agent may choose a low-dose sedating antidepressant (e.g., doxepin for sleep maintenance) or a non-benzodiazepine hypnotic per formulary, but must still complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks and provide counseling on short-term use and safety.

Start with: “He keeps having sudden jerks and blank spells, and school says he’s getting worse.” Volunteer: many daily sudden whole-body/arm jerks that make him drop things; brief staring spells for a few seconds then back to normal; he’s behind in learning/talking; big meltdowns with noise/routine changes; poor sleep.

required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults) and/or very frequent large-volume urination
Inappropriately dilute urine: low urine osmolality (often <300 mOsm/kg, frequently <100–200) and low urine specific gravity (often <1.005)
High-normal or elevated serum osmolality (often >295 mOsm/kg) with high-normal or elevated serum sodium (often ≥145 mmol/L) in the absence of hyperglycemia
Normal or near-normal renal function (creatinine not markedly elevated) to exclude renal failure as the cause of polyuria
Fasting serum glucose not elevated enough to explain osmotic diuresis (rules out uncontrolled diabetes mellitus as primary cause of polyuria)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (often ≥145 mmol/L if free water intake does not fully compensate)
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute (typically <300 mOsm/kg; often <100–200 in more severe cases)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (>3 L/day in adults; can be markedly higher)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (fasting): Normal or not sufficiently elevated to account for osmotic diuresis
expected_lab_results.BUN: Low-normal (may be low due to dilution) unless dehydration present
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal or near-normal (no significant renal impairment)

non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water; drink to thirst (unless impaired thirst or altered mental status)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS; e.g., CANDLE syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: CANDLE syndrome (chronic atypical neutrophilic dermatosis with lipodystrophy and elevated temperature)
required_evidence: Recurrent or persistent systemic inflammation with elevated acute phase reactants (ESR/CRP) and hyperferritinemia
Chronic inflammatory phenotype with cutaneous and/or musculoskeletal involvement (e.g., panniculitis-like lesions, arthralgia/arthritis) consistent with PRAAS
CBC pattern compatible with chronic inflammation (e.g., anemia of inflammation, leukocytosis/neutrophilia, thrombocytosis) in the absence of an alternative infectious explanation

Exclusion of key differentials based on clinical pattern (no primary scleroderma pattern such as progressive skin thickening/Raynaud predominant course; no chilblain-lupus dominant cold-induced acral lesions with lupus serologies; no MONA pattern of progressive osteolysis with nodulosis; no systemic sclerosis organ pattern)
If available/when pursued: supportive genetic confirmation (proteasome/immunoproteasome gene variants such as PSMB8/PSMB4/PSMA3/PSMB9) or specialist confirmation

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low or low-normal (anemia of chronic inflammation is common)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal to low-normal (often normocytic; can be mildly microcytic with inflammation)

medications: Baricitinib 2 mg PO daily (titrate to 4 mg PO daily as tolerated/needed for inflammatory control; specialist-directed off-label for PRAAS)
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg PO daily as short-term bridge for flare control, then taper to the lowest effective dose
Naproxen 250-500 mg PO twice daily with food as needed for pain/inflammation (if no contraindications)
non_pharmacological: Rheumatology/immunology referral for confirmation, genetic testing coordination, and long-term disease control strategy
Vaccination review prior to immunosuppression; avoid live vaccines while on significant immunosuppression/JAK inhibitor per specialist guidance
Infection risk mitigation counseling (prompt evaluation for fever, avoid sick contacts when possible)
Bone protection if prolonged glucocorticoids anticipated (calcium/vitamin D; consider bisphosphonate per risk assessment) and monitor growth/development if pediatric
Screen for and manage cardiometabolic complications and lipodystrophy-related issues if present (nutrition counseling; endocrinology as needed)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks after initiation/adjustment of anti-inflammatory therapy (earlier—within 1 week—if severe symptoms or adverse effects), then every 1-3 months once stable with labs and symptom monitoring.

must_include: This is an autoinflammatory condition driven by innate immune overactivation (often interferon-mediated) rather than typical infection
Why the listed differentials are less likely based on the overall pattern and inflammation markers
Expectation of chronic/relapsing course and need for specialist-led long-term management
Goal: reduce inflammation, prevent organ damage, improve symptoms and quality of life
Rationale for JAK inhibitor therapy (targets interferon signaling) and that use may be off-label and specialist-supervised
Role of steroids as short-term bridge and importance of tapering to minimize side effects
Monitoring plan: symptom response plus inflammatory labs (ESR/CRP/ferritin, CBC) and adverse-effect surveillance
Infection risk with immunosuppressive therapy and when to seek urgent care (fever, shortness of breath, severe new pain, signs of thrombosis)
Potential medication adverse effects (steroid metabolic/bone effects; JAK inhibitor risks including infection, cytopenias, liver/lipid changes, thrombosis risk—discuss individualized risk)
Shared decision-making and confirming patient/family understanding
Vaccination and infection-prevention behaviors
Adherence importance and not stopping steroids abruptly
General anti-inflammatory supportive measures: sleep, nutrition, activity as tolerated; bone health measures if on steroids

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment goals/rationale and monitoring, address concerns (infection/VTE warning signs, adverse effects), and lifestyle/vaccination guidance; confirm understanding and shared decision-making.

Arrange close follow-up for symptom and lab response and adverse-effect surveillance; coordinate specialist referral/ongoing management; set timeline for repeat CBC/CMP/ESR/CRP/ferritin and lipid monitoring if JAK inhibitor used.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are anxious but communicate clearly and politely. Be reluctant/embarrassed unless asked gently: spotting after sex, occasional blood in stool after straining, fear of being seen as dramatic about periods. Express misconceptions: wonder about hemophilia (aunt suggested), fear joint bleeding due to aches, suspect fish oil/garlic or low platelets. correct_diagnosis.primary: Combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII (F5F8D)
required_evidence: Personal and/or family history of mucocutaneous bleeding (epistaxis, easy bruising, menorrhagia) and/or excessive bleeding with procedures
Coagulation testing showing both prolonged PT and prolonged aPTT (not explained by anticoagulant use), with correction on mixing study
Specific factor assays demonstrating reduced factor V activity and reduced factor VIII activity
Normal platelet count (distinguishes from thrombocytopenic causes); platelet function testing may be needed to exclude Glanzmann if suspected clinically
No delayed re-bleeding pattern typical of factor XIII deficiency (in FXIII deficiency PT/aPTT are usually normal)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be low if chronic blood loss (e.g., menorrhagia/epistaxis) has caused anemia; otherwise can be normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: May be low in proportion to hemoglobin if anemia from bleeding is present
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Often low/low-normal if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal (helps distinguish from platelet number disorders)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal (nonspecific; not expected to be altered by F5F8D)
expected_lab_results.Iron studies - Ferritin: May be low if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding is present
expected_lab_results.Iron studies - Serum iron: May be low if iron deficiency is present
expected_lab_results.Iron studies - TIBC: May be high if iron deficiency is present

medications: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.3 mcg/kg IV over 20-30 min once, 30-60 min before minor procedures or for mild bleeding (if prior response documented or after test dose)
Tranexamic acid 25 mg/kg PO (max 1.5 g) every 8 hours for 5-7 days for mucosal bleeding or peri-dental procedures (if no contraindication such as active thrombosis)
Factor VIII concentrate 25-50 IU/kg IV, repeat every 12-24 hours as needed for significant bleeding or perioperative coverage (target levels per procedure severity)
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 10-20 mL/kg IV for clinically significant bleeding or perioperative coverage to replace factor V (repeat guided by clinical response and PT/aPTT)
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day (or 1 tab daily if tolerated) for iron deficiency anemia, continue for ~3 months after hemoglobin normalizes
non_pharmacological: Hematology referral for confirmation with PT/aPTT, mixing study, and factor V and VIII activity levels; consider genetic counseling (autosomal recessive)
Develop an individualized bleeding management plan and peri-procedural protocol; provide medical alert identification
Avoid aspirin and nonselective NSAIDs due to increased bleeding risk; use acetaminophen for pain/fever unless contraindicated
For suspected/confirmed iron deficiency: dietary iron optimization and evaluation of bleeding sources (e.g., heavy menstrual bleeding management in coordination with gynecology)
Vaccinate for hepatitis A and B if likely to receive plasma products (per local practice and risk assessment)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks for diagnostic confirmation and formal management plan; sooner (same day/24-48 h) if active bleeding or pre-procedure planning is needed

must_include: This is a rare inherited bleeding disorder where both factor V and factor VIII are low, increasing bleeding risk
It is typically autosomal recessive; family members may be carriers; offer counseling/testing discussion
Different from classic hemophilia (usually only factor VIII or IX affected) and from platelet disorders (platelet count is normal)
Treatment depends on severity and situation: DDAVP may raise factor VIII in some patients; factor VIII concentrate treats factor VIII deficiency; FFP is used to replace factor V
Antifibrinolytics (e.g., tranexamic acid) help with nose/mouth/uterine bleeding and around dental work
Before surgeries/dental procedures, a hematology-guided plan is needed to prevent bleeding
Review bleeding warning signs requiring urgent care (uncontrolled bleeding, head injury, severe headache, blood in stool/urine, large painful swelling suggesting muscle/joint bleed)
Discuss potential side effects: DDAVP can cause low sodium/fluid retention; tranexamic acid is avoided with active clotting; FFP can cause allergic reactions/volume overload
Confirm penicillin allergy (urticaria) and ensure it is documented for future treatments
Avoid aspirin and nonselective NSAIDs; check with clinicians before starting new OTC meds or supplements
Use medical alert identification and inform all clinicians/dentists about the bleeding disorder
If iron deficiency is present, explain iron therapy adherence and expected timeline for improvement

Interpret results to confirm combined low factor V and factor VIII with otherwise normal platelet count; assess anemia/iron deficiency and degree of coagulation abnormality.
Make/confirm diagnosis of combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII and document severity and clinical phenotype.

Before prescribing any medication, verify and document penicillin allergy (urticaria) and any other drug/FFP reactions.
If prescribing (e.g., tranexamic acid, DDAVP, iron), review interactions with current meds (including hormonal therapy, anticoagulants/antiplatelets) and renal risk factors; noncritical but expected.

Arrange hematology follow-up, genetic counseling/testing discussion, and lab monitoring (e.g., sodium after DDAVP, hemoglobin/ferritin after iron).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected mucolipidosis: age of onset, developmental trajectory, recurrent ENT/pulmonary infections, snoring/OSA symptoms, feeding issues, pain, joint stiffness/contractures, prior surgeries/anesthesia issues, family history/consanguinity, and current medications.
Screen for red flags needing urgent escalation (respiratory distress, cyanosis, inability to feed, lethargy, high fever, severe sleep apnea symptoms).

Initiate diagnostic workup and referrals: lysosomal enzyme panel as appropriate, genetic testing (targeted panel/exome per local protocol), urine GAGs (often normal/variable in mucolipidosis but may help differential), baseline CBC/CMP, and request coordination with metabolic genetics.

Order baseline surveillance studies tied to common complications: echocardiogram/ECG, pulmonary evaluation (sleep study if OSA symptoms, PFTs if feasible), audiology, ophthalmology, skeletal survey/orthopedics evaluation, dental evaluation.
Review any available prior imaging/labs/consult notes to support suspected diagnosis and establish baseline organ involvement.
State working diagnosis (suspected mucolipidosis/lysosomal storage disorder) and differential; clarify that subtype confirmation requires specialized enzyme/genetic testing.
Explain diagnosis in family-centered language, including inheritance, multisystem involvement, and the need for genetics/metabolic coordination.
Explain treatment approach: supportive care, proactive surveillance, infection management, airway/cardiac/orthopedic risk mitigation, and pain control principles.
Address concerns about prognosis variability by subtype (ML II vs ML III), red-flag symptoms, genetic counseling, psychosocial supports, and resources.
Lifestyle and safety education: PT/OT, nutrition/bone health, vaccines/infection prevention, and medication safety including documenting/communicating allergy to amoxicillin-clavulanate.
Required safety check before any prescribing; confirm allergy details (urticarial rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate) and document to avoid re-exposure.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/supplements for interactions with proposed symptomatic therapies (e.g., analgesics, antibiotics).
If symptomatic treatment is needed (e.g., pain/fever), prescribe with safe pediatric dosing guidance and avoidance of contraindicated agents; avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to allergy.
Arrange timely follow-up with metabolic genetics and multidisciplinary surveillance (cardiac, pulmonary/sleep, ENT, orthopedics, PT/OT, audiology/vision/dental) and provide clear return precautions.
Close the encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old daycare assistant, anxious but clear. Start with: “I’ve had really bad diarrhea and stomach cramps since yesterday.” Volunteer early: frequent watery diarrhea (even overnight), strong lower-belly cramping, feeling feverish/wiped out, and urgent need to go even when little comes out.

If asked about stool, fever, or other symptoms: mention blood and slime, nausea/low appetite, chills with home temp 101–102°F, pain/straining when trying to poop, mild headache/body aches, dry mouth, and lightheadedness standing.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about hydration/urination or sick contacts: very little dark urine, cracked lips, a few pounds lost, pain briefly improves after bowel movements, and a child/roommate had a similar bug.

Be reluctant/embarrassed unless the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks directly: anal soreness/leakage, recent sex with anal contact, and poor handwashing after changing a diaper.

Express misconceptions: worry this is Crohn’s/colitis because of blood; fear ibuprofen “burned” your stomach. If clinician shows empathy, you open up more and ask for the cheapest plan, worry about missing work, and ask if you can stop antibiotics early to save pills.

medications: Norfloxacin 400 mg PO twice daily
Succimer 10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours (chelation course)
Oral rehydration solution 250 mL PO after each loose stool
confounders: Recent travel with exposure to untreated water (raises concern for other infectious diarrheas such as giardiasis/ETEC)
Recent antibiotic exposure (risk of C. difficile colitis presenting with diarrhea and leukocytosis)
Microcytic anemia from lead toxicity (may be misattributed to GI blood loss or chronic disease)
Dehydration with electrolyte abnormalities (can mimic systemic illness severity and complicate assessment)

differentials: neonatal inflammatory skin and bowel disease
inflammatory bowel disease, immunodeficiency, and encephalopathy

acceptable_alternatives: Acute infectious colitis due to Shigella species
required_evidence: Acute onset diarrhea, typically frequent small-volume stools
Blood and/or mucus in stool with tenesmus and crampy abdominal pain
Fever or systemic symptoms consistent with invasive bacterial enteritis
Epidemiologic risk (daycare/outbreak, travel, contaminated food/water, close-contact transmission)
No chronic relapsing course or extraintestinal features suggesting inflammatory bowel disease
Clinical picture not consistent with amoebiasis (often subacute, travel to endemic areas) or neonatal inflammatory skin and bowel disease (neonatal onset with dermatitis/immune dysregulation)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis) in moderate/severe disease; may be normal in mild cases
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly decreased if significant bloody diarrhea or dehydration-related hemoconcentration is not present
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal; may be mildly elevated as an acute-phase reactant
expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: May be low-normal or mildly decreased with diarrheal losses
expected_lab_results.BMP - Potassium: May be low (hypokalemia) with ongoing diarrhea
expected_lab_results.BMP - Chloride: Often normal to mildly increased depending on volume status
expected_lab_results.BMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): May be decreased (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis) from bicarbonate loss in diarrhea

medications: Oral rehydration solution as directed PO frequent small amounts (or IV normal saline per severity) until euvolemic
Norfloxacin 400 mg PO twice daily for 3 days (use only if adult and local susceptibility supports; adjust based on culture/susceptibility when available)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3,000 mg/day unless otherwise indicated)
non_pharmacological: Hydration strategy: oral rehydration; IV fluids if unable to tolerate PO, severe dehydration, or hemodynamic instability
Avoid antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide, diphenoxylate) in dysentery/invasive diarrhea due to risk of worsening disease
Hand hygiene and contact precautions; avoid food preparation for others until at least 48 hours after diarrhea resolves (and follow local public health guidance)
Return precautions: worsening abdominal pain, persistent high fever, signs of dehydration, inability to keep fluids down, bloody stools increasing, confusion/neurologic symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24–48 hours (sooner if worsening); review stool testing/culture and adjust antibiotics per susceptibility

must_include: Likely bacterial infection of the colon consistent with shigellosis
How it spreads (fecal-oral, very contagious) and implications for household/daycare contacts
Why differentials like inflammatory bowel disease or amoebiasis are less likely given an acute infectious presentation (and what would prompt reconsideration)
Primary focus on rehydration and electrolyte replacement
Antibiotic purpose: shorten illness and reduce transmission; may be adjusted once susceptibilities are known
Avoid antimotility medications in bloody/inflammatory diarrhea
Expected course and timeline for improvement (often within 48–72 hours with appropriate care)
Review penicillin allergy and clarify reaction history; explain that norfloxacin is not a penicillin
Discuss potential fluoroquinolone adverse effects (tendon pain/rupture risk, neuropathy/CNS effects, QT risk) and to stop/seek care if these occur
Explain red flags requiring urgent evaluation (severe dehydration, persistent high fever, worsening bloody diarrhea, confusion/encephalopathy symptoms)
Strict handwashing after bathroom/diaper changes and before food handling
Hydration guidance and diet as tolerated; avoid alcohol and high-risk foods while ill
Guidance on return to work/school and preventing spread to close contacts

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for acute infectious diarrhea: onset/duration, stool frequency/character (blood/mucus), fever, abdominal cramps/tenesmus, dehydration symptoms, exposures (travel, daycare, sick contacts, food/water), immune status, pregnancy, comorbidities, and current meds.

Clarify severity and risk features that change workup/management: inability to keep fluids, orthostasis, altered mental status, high fever, severe abdominal pain, very young/elderly, immunocompromised.
Order stool testing appropriate for suspected dysentery/shigellosis: stool culture/PCR and susceptibility testing; consider CBC/BMP if dehydrated or systemically ill.
Review results to support diagnosis and guide antibiotic choice (local resistance patterns/susceptibilities).
Make diagnosis of shigellosis (or probable shigellosis pending confirmation) and document reasoning and differentials.
Required safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies and reaction details (especially reported penicillin allergy) to avoid unsafe antibiotic selection.

Required safety check before prescribing: screen for drug-drug interactions relevant to selected therapy (e.g., fluoroquinolones with QT-prolonging agents, polyvalent cations, warfarin; azithromycin QT risk; TMP-SMX interactions).

Collect contraindication-relevant data before prescribing: pregnancy/breastfeeding status, age, history of tendon disorders, myasthenia gravis, known QT prolongation/arrhythmia, seizure disorder, severe renal/hepatic disease.

Initiate appropriate treatment: emphasize oral rehydration; consider antibiotic to shorten illness/reduce transmission when indicated and tailor to susceptibilities; avoid antimotility agents in bloody/inflammatory diarrhea.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/spread/differentials, treatment plan and expected course, safety counseling (fluoroquinolone adverse effects if used), red flags, and lifestyle/return-to-work/school guidance to prevent transmission.
Arrange follow-up within 24–72 hours (or sooner if severe) to reassess hydration, symptom trajectory, and adjust antibiotics based on susceptibilities; advise when to seek urgent care.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding and plan.

minimal_paths.description: Probable shigellosis with dysentery features; start rehydration and empiric antibiotic after safety checks; adjust when susceptibilities return; provide full counseling.

Hold back unless specifically asked: it improves when you avoid sugar 1–2 days, you drink extra water, had a couple cavities recently, family history of “weird sugar problems,” and sometimes drool on the pillow after late dessert.

Be reluctant to admit: carrying a cup to spit, avoiding kissing, constant gum chewing (can worsen it).

Express misconceptions: you suspect reflux, allergies, salivary stone/infection, or diabetes. If clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly and share more. Mention cost/time-off-work worries if plans for tests/meds come up.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old anxious 8th grader. Speak vaguely at first and worry you’re “missing school and sports.” Open with: joints (knees/ankles) swell and hurt on and off but never feel normal; morning stiffness takes a long time; pain after sitting; limp on bad days; you’re tired even with enough sleep.

Only share extra details if asked: wrists/fingers get puffy after writing/gaming; some days you feel warm/achy like getting sick; symptoms worsen when stressed or when you miss medicine; this has happened since you were little; occasional light sensitivity/eye irritation without much redness.

Keep hidden unless directly probed: slower growth, weak grip/opening jars, sore/clicking jaw, heel/foot pain after running, missing classes because mornings are worst.

Resist admitting: you skip PE and say you’re sick from embarrassment; you’re down/snappy but don’t want parents to worry; you don’t take meds consistently because you hate pills and being “the sick kid.”

Express misconceptions: maybe Lyme/ticks, sports injury, or growing pains; gluten-free didn’t help. Assume rheumatology/genetic tests, PT, and long-term meds aren’t covered and “too expensive.” If clinician is empathetic, you soften, get teary, and share more.

medications: Methylprednisolone 8 mg PO daily
Naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily with food
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
Calcium carbonate 500 mg elemental calcium PO twice daily
confounders: Recent upper respiratory viral illness with low-grade fevers (can mimic inflammatory flare and elevate CRP/ESR)
Chronic glucocorticoid therapy (can mask infection signs and contribute to leukocytosis)
NSAID use prior to evaluation (may partially reduce pain/fever and obscure severity)
Microcytic anemia (fatigue may be attributed to anemia rather than systemic inflammation)

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis associated with LACC1 (FAMIN) deficiency (monogenic systemic/early-onset juvenile arthritis)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (≥6 weeks) with recurrent flares
Known pathogenic LACC1 (FAMIN) defect or strong prior genetic documentation
Elevated inflammatory markers during active disease (ESR and/or CRP)
Exclusion of acute bacterial septic arthritis (no single-joint rapidly progressive severe pain with systemic toxicity; labs not consistent with acute bacterial infection)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Mild to moderate leukocytosis during flare (neutrophil predominant) or normal between flares
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild normocytic anemia of chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal (normocytic) or mildly low if chronic inflammation/iron-restricted erythropoiesis
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated (can be markedly elevated if systemic features/macrophage activation risk)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Normal to mildly elevated; monitor for steroid effects and systemic inflammation

medications: Methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for short course (e.g., 5-14 days) then taper to lowest effective dose
Methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) once daily for 1-3 days for severe flare/systemic symptoms, then transition to oral taper
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral/coordination with pediatric rheumatology for steroid-sparing disease control (DMARD/biologic planning) and genetic/monogenic arthritis management
Physical and occupational therapy to maintain range of motion and function; daily gentle ROM exercises
Vaccination review (avoid live vaccines while on high-dose systemic steroids; update inactivated vaccines as appropriate)
Bone health measures if prolonged steroids anticipated (adequate calcium/vitamin D; consider bone protection strategy per specialist)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after steroid initiation (earlier in 48-72 hours if severe flare) with repeat ESR/CRP and CBC; ongoing rheumatology follow-up every 4-12 weeks depending on activity

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis beginning in childhood and linked to a LACC1 (FAMIN) genetic defect
Flares cause joint pain/swelling and elevated inflammation labs; goal is control of inflammation to prevent joint damage and disability
Need to rule out infection if a single joint becomes acutely very painful/hot with high fever or the child looks very ill
Methylprednisolone is being used to quickly reduce inflammation; it is not a long-term solution due to side effects
Plan includes tapering steroids and working with pediatric rheumatology on steroid-sparing therapy (DMARD/biologic) for long-term control
Monitoring plan: repeat CBC/ESR/CRP and watch for signs of systemic complications
Review common steroid adverse effects: mood/behavior changes, sleep disturbance, increased appetite/weight gain, elevated blood pressure/glucose, infection risk, stomach irritation
Red flags requiring urgent care: high fever, severe localized joint pain/swelling, shortness of breath, easy bruising/bleeding, severe abdominal pain, confusion, or rapidly worsening condition
Discuss infection precautions and when to call the care team
Encourage regular low-impact activity as tolerated and adherence to PT/OT exercises
Nutrition guidance to mitigate steroid effects (balanced diet, limit excess sugars/salt; ensure calcium/vitamin D intake)
Medication adherence and not stopping steroids abruptly without a taper plan

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 44-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve had burning when I pee and I’m going to the bathroom all the time.” Volunteer: burning/stinging urination, frequent urination, urgency with little output, and lower belly pressure/discomfort. If asked, add: stronger urine smell, feeling not fully empty, a couple times of pink when wiping, and feeling chilled/tired but you haven’t checked a temperature. Do not volunteer that you’ve been holding urine at work, drinking less water, or used a new scented ‘feminine wash’—only mention if specifically asked about habits, hydration, or products. Be reluctant about sexual history: only disclose a new partner and inconsistent condom use if directly asked; mention slight unusual discharge only if asked about discharge/STIs. Share your misconception: you thought it was a yeast infection and tried cranberry juice and OTC UTI pills without much improvement. Emphasize cost/time concerns: prefer cheapest testing, minimal visits, generic meds, and avoid time off work. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

required_evidence: Acute onset vomiting and/or diarrhea with signs of volume depletion (thirst, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output, orthostatic symptoms)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 24-48 hours (earlier if worsening); same-day evaluation if unable to maintain hydration or significant red flags

It happens a few times a week, usually late morning or if I skip a meal.
During it I get sweaty, my heart races, and I feel like I might pass out.
if_asked: Sometimes I get blurry vision or a headache right before it starts.

It can also happen after I eat a big carb-heavy meal, like pasta or pancakes.
I’ve gained about 10 pounds in the last few months because I keep snacking to prevent it.

My partner says I sometimes seem ‘out of it’ in the morning until I eat.
If I don’t eat quickly during an episode, I can’t think straight enough to drive safely.
I’ve started carrying candy everywhere without really thinking why.
resistant: I sometimes drink a lot on weekends, and the next morning the shaky/confused thing is more likely to happen.
I’ve taken a couple of my friend’s diabetes pills once or twice to try to lose weight (I know that was dumb).
I’m embarrassed to say I’ve been eating in secret because I’m scared of getting another episode.
misleading: I looked it up and I think it might be panic attacks because my heart pounds and I get sweaty.
I thought maybe it’s low iron because I feel weak sometimes.
I’ve been blaming caffeine because it feels like jitters.
noise: My knees have been a bit achy after starting a new workout routine.
I’ve had some seasonal allergies with a runny nose this week.
I get occasional heartburn after spicy food.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying your hips and lower back have been hurting more over time and you feel stiff when moving. Volunteer: aching most days (worse after walking/standing), morning stiffness that takes time to loosen, lifelong short neck, shorter than family, stopped growing early. If asked, add: hips feel tight with reduced ability to spread legs, stairs/low chairs harder, occasional knee aches after activity, fatigue with long walks, told your back looks a bit curved/posture off, intermittent joint pain since teens/early adulthood. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about sexual function: sex is uncomfortable in some hip positions so you’ve been avoiding it. If asked about other details, disclose: occasional sharp groin pinch with twisting/stepping wrong, fear of hip “catching,” ROM slowly shrinking, occasional neck stiffness/headaches after computer work; hands/fingers fine. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and briefly share worries about genetic diagnoses affecting insurance and costs of imaging/PT/specialists.

Defers prescribing and instead provides a detailed OTC plan and PT referral guidance; still performs required safety checks before recommending specific NSAID dosing.
